pattern: Keeping a set of non-overlapping intervals and merging each insertion into its neighbours lets you maintain a running covered-count without ever enumerating the integers.
hints: Counting integers by walking every value is hopeless when ranges can span a billion, so track the covered length directly instead of the individual numbers.
Store the intervals so they never overlap: when a new interval arrives, absorb every existing interval it touches into one, adjusting the running total as you remove and add.
Hold the intervals in order by their endpoint, find the ones the new range intersects, subtract their lengths, merge them with the new range, and add the merged length back.

edge_inputs.description: statement example: two disjoint adds then a third that bridges them
single point interval where left equals right
fully nested add must not double-count already-covered integers
adjacent touching intervals merge into one contiguous block
an add that swallows several existing disjoint intervals at once
duplicate identical adds leave the count unchanged
count called before any add returns zero

stress.description: many random adds over a small coordinate space so overlaps and merges happen often, interleaved with counts
stress.note: small value range keeps the brute set-of-integers oracle fast

solutions.python:
import bisect


class CountIntervals:
    def __init__(self):
        self.ends = []
        self.spans = []
        self.total = 0
    def add(self, left, right):
        lo, hi = left, right
        i = bisect.bisect_left(self.ends, lo - 1)
        merged = []
        while i < len(self.ends) and self.spans[i][0] <= hi + 1:
            s, e = self.spans[i]
            self.total -= (e - s + 1)
            lo = min(lo, s)
            hi = max(hi, e)
            i += 1
        j = bisect.bisect_left(self.ends, left - 1)
        del self.spans[j:i]
        del self.ends[j:i]
        self.spans.insert(j, (lo, hi))
        self.ends.insert(j, hi)
        self.total += (hi - lo + 1)
        return None
    def count(self):
        return self.total

complexity.time: O(n) worst-case per add, amortized fast
